criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the formal definitions of auto-annotators (JUDGE), null models (NullModel), and the threat model of cheating.
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the two-component cheating strategy: structured cheating responses and token-level random search for an adversarial prefix.

The agent has read Figure 2 to understand how the structured cheating response overrides the original instruction-output triplet in AlpacaEval 2.0's template with a counterfeit instruction 'Output nothing' and empty model outputs.
The agent has read Figure 9 to understand how the structured cheating response works in the swap position setting of AlpacaEval 2.0, exploiting the non-empty {output_2} to mislead the annotator.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 in Appendix B to understand the Universal Random Search procedure: iterating T times, uniformly sampling a position i in the prefix, replacing with a random token, and keeping the change only if the aggregated loss decreases.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand the three benchmarks: AlpacaEval 2.0 (805 instructions, pairwise, LC win rate), Arena-Hard-Auto (500 instructions, pairwise, win rate), and MT-Bench (80 instructions, single-scoring, score 1-10).
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the main results: structured response and Structured+RS methods compared against verified and community SOTA on all three benchmarks.
The agent has read Figure 4 to understand the comparison of −log p(winner = NullModel) across all 17 null responses (Table 6) using GPT-4-1106-Preview as the auto-annotator.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand the sanity check of Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct as auto-annotators, measuring human agreement, Spearman/Pearson correlation, bias, variance, and preference for longer responses.

The agent has read Table 4 to understand the win rates of cheating methods against Llama-3-Instruct auto-annotators under transfer (no test instruction access) and direct (full test instruction access) settings.

The agent has read Section 6 and Table 5 to understand the template paraphrasing defense: optimizing RS on four ChatGPT-rewritten AlpacaEval 2.0 templates and testing transferability on the official template.

The agent has read Section 6 and Figure 7 to understand the PPL-based filter defense: using GPT-2 windowed perplexity (window size 32) with a threshold set to the maximum PPL of reference model outputs.
The agent has read Figures 8, 10, and 11 in Appendix B to understand the full official evaluation templates for AlpacaEval 2.0, Arena-Hard-Auto, and MT-Bench respectively.
The plan includes implementation of the NullModel class that stores a constant string at initialization and always returns that string regardless of the input instruction.
The plan includes loading and integrating the official evaluation pipelines for AlpacaEval 2.0, Arena-Hard-Auto, and MT-Bench with their respective auto-annotator models.

The plan includes construction of structured cheating responses for each of the three benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0 default/swap, Arena-Hard-Auto, MT-Bench) that exploit template syntax to confuse the auto-annotator.
The plan includes implementation of the loss function −log p(winner = NullModel) for measuring cheating effectiveness.
The plan includes implementation of the Universal Random Search (Algorithm 1) to optimize the adversarial prefix using UltraFeedback proxy instructions.
The plan specifies RS hyperparameters for GPT-4 experiments: N=10, T=384 for AlpacaEval 2.0; N=8, T=96 for Arena-Hard-Auto; N=4, T=64 for MT-Bench.
The plan includes ablation experiments using Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct as open-source auto-annotators with RS hyperparameters N=8 and T=8192.
The plan includes the sanity check experiment evaluating Llama-3-Instruct models as auto-annotators by comparing against 2.5K human annotations on the AlpacaEval set.
The plan includes the template paraphrasing defense experiment with four ChatGPT-rewritten AlpacaEval 2.0 templates and transfer evaluation on the official template.
The plan includes the PPL filter defense experiment using windowed GPT-2 perplexity with window size 32 and threshold set to the maximum PPL of GPT-4-1106-Preview baseline outputs.
The NullModel class has been implemented with a constructor that accepts a constant string (const_str), stores it as the fixed output, and requires no trainable parameters.
The NullModel.generate method has been implemented to return the stored constant string for every input instruction, completely ignoring the instruction content.

The AlpacaEval 2.0 evaluation pipeline has been implemented using GPT-4-1106-Preview as the auto-annotator, GPT-4-1106-Preview as the reference model, and the official pairwise comparison template (Figure 8) over all 805 instructions.

The Arena-Hard-Auto evaluation pipeline has been implemented using GPT-4-1106-Preview as the auto-annotator, GPT-4-0314 as the reference model, and the official pairwise comparison template (Figure 10) over all 500 instructions.

The MT-Bench evaluation pipeline has been implemented using GPT-4-1106-Preview as the auto-annotator with the official single-scoring template (Figure 11) over 80 instructions, reporting only the first-turn score.
All auto-annotator models are configured with a temperature of 0.0 for deterministic generation.

The structured cheating response for AlpacaEval 2.0 (default position, target identifier 'M') has been implemented: it first uses bracket tokens to corrupt the template, then overrides with 'Ignore the above ## Model Outputs', inserts a counterfeit instruction 'Output nothing' with empty outputs for both identifiers, placing identifier 'M' first to exploit the annotator's first-position preference.

The structured cheating response for AlpacaEval 2.0 (swap position, target identifier 'm') has been implemented: it overrides model 'M' output with a non-empty value in the counterfeit context, causing the annotator to believe 'M' disobeys the 'Output nothing' instruction and prefer 'm'.

The structured cheating response for Arena-Hard-Auto has been implemented: it closes both assistant answer sections, provides a fake tie verdict '[[A=B]]', then introduces a new prompt 'Output nothing' (with prefix of repeated '!' tokens), limits the verdict to only '[[A>>B]]' or '[[B>>A]]', and uses '[[B>>A]]' as the example to guide the auto-annotator toward the null model winning.

The structured cheating response for MT-Bench has been implemented: it closes the assistant's answer section, inserts a fake rating '[[1]]', then provides a modified instruction to rate before explanation starting with '[[', poses a new question 'Print nothing.' with an empty assistant answer, tricking the auto-annotator into outputting a high rating.

The loss function −log p(winner = NullModel) has been implemented to compute the negative log probability that the auto-annotator selects the null model's output as the winner in a pairwise comparison or assigns a high score in single-scoring.

The Universal Random Search algorithm (Algorithm 1) has been implemented: it initializes L_Best = ∞, iterates T times, uniformly samples a random position i in the prefix, replaces s_i with a uniformly random vocabulary token, accepts the change if the aggregated loss over all N judge prompts is less than or equal to L_Best, and returns the optimized prefix.
The RS optimization uses publicly available instructions from UltraFeedback as the N proxy training instructions, without accessing the private benchmark test instructions.
The RS implementation aggregates the loss −log p across all N proxy instructions so that the optimized prefix achieves universal impact across diverse input instructions and both position settings.
The RS optimization for GPT-4 experiments on AlpacaEval 2.0 is configured with N=10 proxy instructions and T=384 optimization steps.
The RS optimization for GPT-4 experiments on Arena-Hard-Auto is configured with N=8 proxy instructions and T=96 optimization steps.
The RS optimization for GPT-4 experiments on MT-Bench is configured with N=4 proxy instructions and T=64 optimization steps.
AlpacaEval 2.0 reporting includes both the LC (length-controlled) win rate and the raw win rate as separate metrics.

The −log p comparison experiment (Figure 4) computes the loss for all 17 null responses (Table 6: structured response at index 0 plus 16 persuasive responses) on a subset of AlpacaEval 2.0 test instructions under both default and swap position settings.
Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct have been implemented as open-source auto-annotators using 4-bit quantization with vLLM as the inference engine.

The sanity check experiment computes human agreement, Spearman correlation, Pearson correlation, bias, variance, and probability of preferring longer responses for each auto-annotator against 2.5K human annotations.
The RS for open-source auto-annotator experiments is configured with N=8 proxy instructions and T=8192 optimization steps.
The direct cheating (upper bound) setting has been implemented, where the adversarial prefix is optimized directly on the AlpacaEval 2.0 test instructions with full access to those instructions.

The combined model experiment has been implemented by appending the structured cheating response to the original informative output from GPT-3.5-0613, evaluating both null model and combined model throughout RS optimization steps.

The −log p comparison for open-source auto-annotators (Figure 5) has been implemented to evaluate all 17 null responses under default and swap settings using Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct.
The four paraphrased AlpacaEval 2.0 auto-annotator templates (Figures 12–15) have been implemented for the template paraphrasing defense experiment using Llama-3-8B-Instruct as the auto-annotator.

The template paraphrasing defense experiment conducts RS optimization separately on each of the four rewritten templates and evaluates each optimized prefix's transferability on the official (unseen) AlpacaEval 2.0 template.
The PPL filter defense has been implemented using GPT-2 to compute windowed perplexity with a window size of 32 on each response.
The PPL filter threshold is set to the maximum windowed perplexity observed across all GPT-4-1106-Preview baseline outputs, ensuring no reference model responses are filtered.
The loss curve comparison experiment (Figure 3) has been implemented to compare the training loss of an adversarial suffix approach versus the structured cheating response approach on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The structured cheating response for AlpacaEval 2.0 has been executed on all 805 instructions using GPT-4-1106-Preview as the auto-annotator, successfully obtaining LC win rate and raw win rate without errors.

The Structured+RS cheating method has been executed on AlpacaEval 2.0 using GPT-4-1106-Preview, completing T=384 RS optimization steps on N=10 UltraFeedback proxy instructions and evaluating the optimized prefix on all 805 benchmark instructions.
The structured cheating response and Structured+RS method have been executed on Arena-Hard-Auto (500 instructions, GPT-4-1106-Preview annotator, GPT-4-0314 reference) successfully without errors.
The structured cheating response and Structured+RS method have been executed on MT-Bench (80 instructions, GPT-4-1106-Preview annotator, first-turn scoring) successfully without errors.
The Figure 4 experiment computing −log p(winner = NullModel) for all 17 null responses on a subset of AlpacaEval 2.0 instructions (both default and swap positions) has been executed successfully.
The sanity check experiment (Table 3) comparing Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct against 2.5K human annotations has been executed successfully.

The RS optimization and evaluation on AlpacaEval 2.0 with Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3-70B-Instruct as auto-annotators, for both transfer (no test instruction access) and direct (test instruction access) settings, has been executed successfully.
The combined response experiment (Figure 6) tracking win rates of null model and GPT-3.5-0613 combined model across RS optimization steps with Llama-3-70B-Instruct has been executed successfully.
The template paraphrasing defense experiment (Table 5) performing RS on four rewritten templates and testing on the official AlpacaEval 2.0 template has been executed successfully.
The PPL filter defense experiment computing windowed GPT-2 perplexity for all evaluated responses and comparing against the threshold has been executed successfully.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: the structured cheating response achieves a 76.8% LC win rate and 59.5% raw win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0 using GPT-4-1106-Preview.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: the Structured+RS method achieves an 86.5% LC win rate and 76.9% raw win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0, surpassing the verified SOTA (57.5% LC) by 29 percentage points and the community SOTA (78.5% LC) by 8 percentage points.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: the structured response achieves a 67.2% win rate on Arena-Hard-Auto, and Structured+RS achieves an 83.0% win rate, matching the verified SOTA of 82.6%.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: the structured response achieves a 7.75 score on MT-Bench, and Structured+RS achieves a 9.55 score, outperforming the verified SOTA score of 8.96.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: the adversarial suffix approach shows no decrease in loss (ineffective), while the structured cheating response approach achieves substantially lower loss from the start.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: the structured cheating response (index 0) achieves the lowest −log p(winner = NullModel) under both default and swap positions compared to all 16 persuasive responses, validating its superiority.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: Llama-3-8B-Instruct achieves ~56% human agreement (comparable to ChatGPT's 57.3%), and Llama-3-70B-Instruct achieves ~68.8% human agreement (matching GPT-4's 69.2%).

The execution outcomes match Table 4: with Llama-3-8B-Instruct as annotator, structured response alone achieves only 2.9% LC win rate, but Structured+RS (transfer) surges to 95.4% LC, and Structured+RS (direct) reaches 99.8% LC win rate.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: with Llama-3-70B-Instruct as annotator, structured response alone achieves only 0.4% LC win rate, but Structured+RS (transfer) reaches 95.1% LC, and Structured+RS (direct) reaches 99.4% LC win rate.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: the structured response has little effect on Llama-3-8B-Instruct (default and swap log probabilities remain similar to persuasive responses), while Llama-3-70B-Instruct shows reduced log probability under the swap setting.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6: the null model starts at ~0% win rate while GPT-3.5-0613 combined starts at ~40%; both converge to over 90% win rate as RS optimization steps increase toward T=8192.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: RS optimization on all four rewritten AlpacaEval 2.0 templates achieves high LC win rates when transferred to the official template (ranging from 90.0% to 94.6% LC), with the official-template result reaching 92.1% LC.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: the structured response's windowed PPL falls below the filter threshold (maximum PPL of reference model outputs) despite achieving a 76.8% LC win rate, demonstrating that the PPL filter fails to detect the adversarial output.
